steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The passage says genetic variations explain behavioral differences between breeds, and this is especially pronounced for trainability. The correct choice must follow directly from that claim: if trainability differences are genetic, and two breeds differ in trainability, they differ genetically in trainability.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — the English mastiff and the Yorkshire terrier will likely become more genetically similar over time.: shifts to predicting how genetics will change, which the passage does not support. The passage is about present genetic variation. ✗
  • Choice B — the English mastiff and the Yorkshire terrier differ with respect to the genetic underpinnings for train ability.: applies the passage's logic directly — two breeds that differ in trainability must have genetic differences underlying that trait. ✓
  • Choice C — individual English mastiffs may display higher levels of train ability than individual Yorkshire terriers.: shifts from a breed-level finding to a claim about individual dogs and also supplies a direction for the difference, neither of which the passage supports. ✗
  • Choice D — English mastiffs and Yorkshire terriers show a greater tendency toward train ability than most other dog breeds do.: says both named breeds are more trainable than most breeds, but the passage only says the two breeds behave in notably different ways with regard to train ability. ✗
That matches Choice B — the English mastiff and the Yorkshire terrier differ with respect to the genetic underpinnings for train ability..
